abstract: From the Arab Spring to more recent protest movements in the EU, Russia, and Turkey, modern uprisings are frequently characterized as "Twitter revolutions." This label highlights the decisive role of new media, the speed of international coverage, and the resulting cycle of global reactions. Yet, this dynamic is not entirely modern; with the rise of the printing press, early-modern revolts faced a remarkably similar media revolution. The introduction of print fundamentally altered the trajectory of these conflicts, which often escalated into full-scale propaganda wars once insurgents gained access to the press. Beyond the battlefield, this shift also transformed the way revolts were narrated. Because domestic governments strictly censored and persecuted dissident views, local accounts were often stifled. In contrast, observers across borders—free from such local allegiances—were able to reflect more openly on these events. Consequently, this book examines how revolts were represented and reimagined across international boundaries, where the absence of censorship allowed for more complex interpretations.
